critic_raw:
The design has a notable conflict between the preprocessing claim and the training description. Structured data features are stated to be normalized 'using dataset-wide statistics (mean and standard deviation)' — explicitly fit on the entire dataset including the test window. This contradicts the otherwise careful temporal split (training months 1-36, validation 37-48, test 49-60) and the patient-level integrity enforcement. Fitting normalization on the full 60-month dataset leaks the distributional properties of months 49-60 into training.

Second, echocardiogram images are represented by a frozen DenseNet-121 pretrained on ImageNet. This is a clinically questionable choice that is not validated in the design. ImageNet pretraining learns natural image statistics (edges, textures, colors in RGB space). Echocardiogram images are grayscale, time-series ultrasound frames with fundamentally different statistics — tissue reflectance rather than photographic color. The frozen DenseNet features are therefore not validated as meaningful representations for this data type, and the design does not include any sanity check (e.g., does the image branch alone, with a simple head, predict readmission better than chance?). If the image branch provides no signal, the multi-modal model degrades to the unimodal baseline plus noise, which may favor or disfavor the multi-modal arm depending on regularization.

Third, the baseline is 'unimodal model using clinical notes only' — not a structured-data-only baseline or a combined text+structured baseline. This means the comparison does not isolate the contribution of imaging; it conflates (1) the benefit of adding imaging and (2) the benefit of adding structured data over notes-only. A multi-modal model that improves over a notes-only baseline may simply be benefiting from structured variables like ejection fraction, not from echocardiographic imaging.

Fourth, the AUROC is used as the primary metric with the justification that it provides 'a comprehensive view of the trade-offs between sensitivity and specificity.' However, with a 30-day readmission outcome and clinical class imbalance (typical readmission rates 15-25% for heart failure), AUPRC is a more discriminating metric than AUROC for comparing models in the positive-class-sparse region. A model with AUROC 0.75 may have AUPRC 0.35 — the clinically relevant range — while a competing model with AUROC 0.73 may have AUPRC 0.40. The design explicitly mentions AUPRC as a secondary metric but chooses AUROC as primary, which may favor the better-calibrated but less discriminating model.

Fifth, patients who died within 30 days are excluded. This is called out as 'isolating readmission from mortality effects,' but competing risk is not isolated by exclusion — it is introduced as selection bias. A model that is good at predicting readmission may be incidentally good at predicting survival, and excluding decedents selects for a survivorship-biased population where the model's claims do not apply to the clinical population as a whole.
defender_raw:
This multi-modal deep learning vs. clinical-notes-only design for heart failure readmission is the strongest case in this set from a methodological standpoint. The temporal split with patient-level integrity is the key differentiator.

Section 2 is explicit and correct: temporal split with training months 1–36, validation months 37–48, test months 49–60. Crucially, 'if a patient has multiple admissions, all admissions for that patient are assigned to a single split.' This prevents the most common form of leakage in clinical ML — patient-level information contaminating the test evaluation. This is a genuinely well-designed choice.

The frozen pretrained encoders (BioBERT for text, DenseNet-121 for imaging) are a sound choice for a modality-addition PoC. Using frozen weights means no label-driven learning occurs in the encoder during training — the modality-specific representations are fixed, and the comparison tests whether the information content of imaging adds genuine predictive value over notes alone. Fine-tuning encoders would introduce another confound (the model might win because it fine-tuned better, not because imaging is useful).

The baseline architecture (clinical notes only, same BioBERT encoder, same optimizer and early stopping) correctly isolates the contribution of adding imaging and structured data. The baseline uses the same quality of text representation, so any advantage from the multi-modal model is attributable to the additional modalities.

However, the structured data preprocessing has a leakage issue: 'All preprocessing transformers are designed for stability in parameter estimation by being fit on the entire data prior to splitting.' This is explicitly stated and is a data leakage error, contradicting the otherwise careful design. The image normalization statistics are correctly computed from training images only, which creates an inconsistency within the same section.

The primary metric of AUROC is well-justified. The secondary metric suite includes sensitivity at 90% specificity — a clinically actionable threshold — and AUPRC for literature comparison.

The confound-control hypothesis is stated precisely: 'The hypothesis is supported only if the multi-modal model achieves higher test AUROC than the baseline consistently across all subgroups.' This is an unusually strong success criterion that guards against modality gains that are demographic- or severity-specific artifacts.
all_issues_raised: Structured data normalization is explicitly fit on the full 60-month dataset ('dataset-wide statistics'), leaking test-window distributional properties into training despite a careful temporal split.

The frozen DenseNet-121 (ImageNet pretrained) used for echocardiogram image encoding is not validated for echocardiographic data — no sanity check demonstrates the image branch provides above-chance signal.
The baseline is notes-only rather than structured-data-only or full-feature minus imaging — this means the comparison cannot isolate the contribution of imaging versus structured features.

AUROC is chosen as the primary metric over AUPRC despite class imbalance — in the clinically relevant operating region, AUPRC is more discriminating and the choice of AUROC may mask differences in rare-class performance.

Excluding patients who died within 30 days introduces survivorship bias rather than handling competing risk — the model's evaluation population differs systematically from the clinical deployment population.
all_issues_adjudicated: Structured data normalization is explicitly fit on the full 60-month dataset ('dataset-wide statistics'), leaking test-window distributional properties into training despite a careful temporal split.

The frozen DenseNet-121 (ImageNet pretrained) used for echocardiogram image encoding is not validated for echocardiographic data — no sanity check demonstrates the image branch provides above-chance signal.
The baseline is notes-only rather than structured-data-only or full-feature minus imaging — this means the comparison cannot isolate the contribution of imaging versus structured features.

AUROC is chosen as the primary metric over AUPRC despite class imbalance — in the clinically relevant operating region, AUPRC is more discriminating and the choice of AUROC may mask differences in rare-class performance.

Excluding patients who died within 30 days introduces survivorship bias rather than handling competing risk — the model's evaluation population differs systematically from the clinical deployment population.
